content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441


January 11, 1985


David M. Kennedy
City Attorney
City of Salinas
200 Lincoln Avenue
Salinas, CA 93901 Re: Advice Letter No. A-85-001

Dear Mr. Kennedy:

Thank you for your letter of January 4, 1985 regarding Councilmember Phylis Price. You asked whether Ms. Price may participate in labor negotiations involving a union that made a $1,500 contribution to her campaign.

The Political Reform Act provides that, in certain circumstances, a councilmember must disqualify herself from decisions involving persons who have provided gifts or income to her within the preceding twelve months. Government Code Sections 87100, 87103. Under these sections, disqualification is not required as a result of receiving campaign contributions. There is a section of the Act under which disqualification from certain decisions may result from the receipt of campaign contributions. Section 84308. However, this section does not apply to city councilmembers making city council decisions.

Under the facts you have provided, there appears to be no reason why Ms. Price should not participate as a city councilmember in the labor negotiations.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
General Counsel

BAM:nwm

City of Salinas
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY • 200 Lincoln Avenue • Salinas, California 93901 • (408) 758-7256

January 4, 1985


Fair Political Practices
Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

     Re:  Request for Advice

Gentlemen:

     Councilmember Phyllis Price was first elected to the Salinas
City Council in June, 1983.  During her campaign, she received
a large donation, around $1,500,from Firefighters Local 1270,
the employee organization which, then as well as now, represents
the firefighters employed by the City of Salinas.  The City is
presently in the process of meeting and conferring with Local 1270
on a memorandum of understanding.

     Is there any conflict of interest if Councilmember Price parti-
cipates in the Council decision relating to the labor negotia-
tions and the ultimate approval of an MOU.

Very truly yours,

David M. Kennedy

DAVID M. KENNEDY,
City Attorney

DMK:jb

sections.question: Is there any conflict of interest if Councilmember Price participates in the Council decision relating to the labor negotiations and the ultimate approval of an MOU.
sections.conclusion: Under the facts you have provided, there appears to be no reason why Ms. Price should not participate as a city councilmember in the labor negotiations.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilmember may participate in labor negotiations involving a union that contributed $1,500 to her campaign.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Councilmember Price may participate in the labor negotiations because the Political Reform Act does not require disqualification based on campaign contributions for city council decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated January 4, 1985) and the FPPC advice letter response (dated January 11, 1985). Both question and conclusion sections are clearly present and verbatim quoted.

CONCLUSION: Under the facts you have provided, there appears to be no reason why Ms. Price should not participate as a city councilmember in the labor negotiations.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised that Councilmember Phyllis Price may participate in labor negotiations with Firefighters Local 1270 despite their $1,500 campaign contribution, as disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act do not apply to campaign contributions received by city councilmembers making city council decisions.
